feedback.text: As a Spanish Citizen, a player for decades and a committed consumer, I am firmly involved in the Stop Killing Games movement and I fully support initiatives such as the Digital Fairness Act, because what is at stake are not just bits or entertainment, but our fundamental rights as consumers in the digital age. For years, I have bought video games with a legitimate expectation of being able to access them whenever you wish to do so, as is the case for any other goods purchased. However, increasingly, large distributors remove digital games from the market, close servers, or block access even to products that have already been paid and downloaded. This is not only deeply unfair: it is a direct violation of consumer rights. When I buy a book, film or disc, no one can come years later to take it away, even if the publisher closes or his author changes his mind. Why should video games be different? Industry cannot rely on the terms of service to justify the removal or removal of content that has already been purchased. Digital cannot be synonymous with the ephemeral. The change in support does not mean that we must lose rights that are already recognised in the consumption of traditional goods. Video games are not just products: these are cultural experiences, stories, interactive works of art. Many of them are invaluable for those we enjoy. By destroying or disabling titles, distributors are not only stealing time and money: they are deleting part of our collective memory. In addition, this practice blocks the preservation of digital cultural heritage, preventing future generations from accessing these works. It is imperative that clear and enforceable laws are put in place to oblige companies to guarantee access to purchased video games, even if servers close or stop supporting them. Solutions exist: from allowing offline use, releasing games for preservation, or providing patches that make it possible to continue playing them without relying on external servers. We do not ask for privileges, just justice. In this context, I welcome the proposal of the Digital Fairness Act, which recognises that digital consumers deserve the same rights as in any other area. This legislation can be a crucial step towards a fairer, more transparent and more respectful market for the video game industry: players. Defending my video games is to defend my freedom to enjoy what I have paid for, my right to keep my digital library and my voice as a consumer. We are not against progress or the digital model, but we are opposed to companies destroying content by unilateral decision, leaving the player defenceless. Video games should not die by business decision. Their value is measured not only in annual profits, but in the vivencies they generate. As a consumer, as a European carer and as a player, I demanded respect, transparency and accountability. And I will not stop raising the voice until we are heard.
